content.problemStatement:
Given a string numnumnum representing a non-negative integer and an integer kkk, remove kkk digits from num to form the smallest possible integer. Return this smallest possible integer as a string.


Input Format

First line contains two integers nnn and kkk , nnn is the size of num and kkk is the number of digits that need to be removed.
Second line contains a string num.


Output Format

Print the smallest possible integer as a string, without leading zero except for the zero itself.
constraints: num consists only of digits '0'-'9' and has no leading zero unless it is "0"
content.inputFormat: First line contains integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers n and k, where n is the length of the number and k is the number of digits to remove. The second line contains a string num of length n representing a non‑negative integer (no leading zeros unless the number itself is zero).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the smallest possible integer after removing exactly k digits from num. The result must not contain leading zeros, except that the number zero is represented by a single "0". Print each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Removing the first '9' and the second '9' yields the smallest possible number 134.
Removing the leading '7' and '8' leaves only zeros; after stripping leading zeros the result is 0.
All digits are equal; removing any one of them leaves the number 111.

solution.approach: The optimal strategy is to scan the digits from left to right while maintaining a stack (or deque) of the currently kept digits. For each new digit, we remove the previous larger digit(s) from the stack as long as we still have deletions left (k > 0). This ensures that the leftmost possible digit is as small as possible, which yields the minimal overall number. After processing all digits, if deletions remain we remove them from the end of the stack. Finally we strip any leading zeros; if the result becomes empty we output "0". The algorithm runs in O(n) time per test case because each digit is pushed and popped at most once, and uses O(n) auxiliary space for the stack.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, where n is the length of the input number. Total O(sum of n over all test cases).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) auxiliary space for the stack/deque.
